description: This figure presents a quantitative plot illustrating the evolution of average grain size as a function of annealing temperature (ranging from 900 °C to 1200 °C) in Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel. The data, obtained from SEM micrographs, displays three distinct curves with error bars representing standard deviations: (1) α₂ phase (banded shape, pink diamonds), representing ferrite that solidified first during casting and distributed continuously with large grains; (2) γ phase (blue triangles), indicating austenite; and (3) α₁ phase (red squares), representing ferrite mainly distributed among γ grains with relatively small grain sizes. The plot reveals that the α₂ ferrite exhibits the largest grain size (increasing from ~26 μm at 900 °C to ~68 μm at 1200 °C) and fastest growth rate with increasing temperature. The γ phase shows moderate grain growth from ~5.5 μm to ~36 μm over the same temperature range. Notably, the α₁ phase displays an initial slight decrease in grain size from 900 °C to 950 °C (from ~3.2 μm to ~2.5 μm) before increasing to ~22 μm at 1200 °C. These grain size variations are identified as the primary factor governing the tensile properties of the steel above 900 °C, following the Hall–Petch relationship, where larger grain sizes correspond to decreased yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, and elongation. The figure distinguishes between the two ferrite morphologies (banded α₂ versus intergranular α₁) to explain the anomalous grain growth behavior compared to conventional Fe–Mn–Al–C steels.
